one_line_takeaway: A conceptual/position note (no experiments) arguing that 'machine unlearning' conflates two distinct goals — removing the influence of specific forget-set examples ('Untraining', which they show is what the standard (ε,δ)-unlearning definition actually formalizes) versus removing an entire underlying concept/behaviour ('Unlearning', their new Definition 7.1) — and that conflating them causes mismatched expectations, inappropriate metrics (e.g., using MIA to evaluate concept removal), and stalled research questions in the unlearning/AI-safety literature.
key_finding: The paper's own claims, not experimental results: (1) the classic/standard (ε,δ)-unlearning definition used throughout the field is actually a formalization of 'Untraining' (removing only the influence of the specific forget-set examples), not of removing the broader concept/behaviour those examples represent. (2) For a forget set of non-memorized examples, or for a model trained with differential privacy, the ideal Untraining solution is a no-op (ideal DP retraining is already 'the same' whether or not the forget examples are included), whereas Unlearning that same forget set would require substantial modification to remove the underlying concept — showing the two problems can have arbitrarily different solutions for the identical forget set S. (3) Metrics appropriate for one problem can be inappropriate for the other: membership inference attack (MIA) success/failure on the forget set is a valid metric for Untraining but not for Unlearning, since an unlearning algorithm could pass an MIA on S while the model retains full knowledge of the underlying distribution S was drawn from (detectable only via MIA on other, non-forget-set examples of that distribution).
core_contribution: This is a conceptual/position note (not an empirical paper) that argues the term 'machine unlearning' has been overloaded in the literature to cover two distinct problem formulations, and proposes a formal disambiguation. The authors define 'Untraining' as the process of reversing empirical risk minimization on a forget set S ⊆ D — i.e., producing a model matching A(D\S), a model retrained from scratch excluding only S — and 'Unlearning' as generalizing beyond S to remove an entire underlying concept/behaviour B, i.e., producing a model matching A(D\S_full) where S_full is the complete set of examples in D exhibiting B (their Definition 7.1). Their central technical claim is that the classic, widely-used (ε,δ)-unlearning definition (Neel et al. 2021, reproduced as the paper's Definition 4.1) is actually a definition of Untraining, not Unlearning as commonly assumed, because it only requires indistinguishability from a model retrained without S, not removal of the broader concept S represents. They support this with a worked example: if S contains only non-memorized (low counterfactual-memorization-score, per Feldman 2020's Definition 3.1) examples, the retrain-from-scratch reference model A(D\S) will still predict those examples correctly (since they weren't memorized), so the 'ideal' Untraining solution requires no change to the model at all — which the authors argue is not what people intuitively mean by 'unlearning a behaviour.'
threat_model: Not applicable — this is a conceptual/definitional note that proposes no attack, defense, evaluation protocol, or empirical threat model of its own. It surveys and categorizes threat-adjacent use cases from cited prior work (privacy/right-to-be-forgotten deletion, membership inference attacks, data poisoning/backdoor removal, dangerous-capability removal in LLMs) purely to illustrate where its Untraining/Unlearning distinction applies; none of these threat models are the paper's own construction.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 10): (1) the note does not offer a complete taxonomy of unlearning algorithms (they point to existing surveys, e.g., Nguyen et al. 2022, for that); (2) it does not address failure modes/inherent limits of post-hoc knowledge/capability removal (citing Cooper et al. 2024, Shumailov et al. 2024, Siddiqui et al. 2026 for that discussion); (3) the delete-vs-suppress distinction from prior work is explicitly called orthogonal to their Untraining/Unlearning axis and not addressed; (4) their Definition 7.1 requires a 'behaviour'/'concept' to be specifiable via a representative example set S_full, which does not hold for all concepts (their own counterexample: 'edge detection' cannot be captured this way) — such concepts are out of scope; (5) in the Q&A section they concede (Q4) that Unlearning as defined is potentially ill-posed unless S perfectly spans the feature space of S_full, since infinitely many models could match on S without matching A(D\S_full); they connect this to the separately-studied 'concept erasure' literature (Amara et al., 2025) but do not resolve it.
